articles.title: Full Writeup of the Windows GDID
articles.summary: The Windows Global Device Identifier is a persistent identifier generated by Microsoft's system for tracking devices on the network, which can be accessed through various means including public APIs and registry reads. This identifier is not derived from hardware serial numbers but rather from information stored in the device graph as a decimal value. The court has confirmed that every claim about the 128-bit nature of the Global Device Identifier is incorrect, and it was generated by reusing login.live.com credentials during a reinstall process.

articles.title: Linux on the Atari Jaguar
articles.summary: The Atari Jaguar console ended up being a commercial disaster despite its promise of high-performance capabilities due in part to its architecture code for the 68000-family processors that is also used by Linux. The Motorola 68000 processor, which was released in 1979 and became widely adopted in commercial hardware, has mixed 16-32 bit capabilities and a 24-bit address bus with a maximum of 16MBs of addressable memory. This architecture code can be found in the Linux kernel's arch/m68k/ directory, making it feasible to port a Linux system to the Jaguar console.

articles.title: A global workspace in language models
articles.summary: A global workspace in language models emerges as a distinct category of internal neural patterns that play a special role in modern language models like Claude. These patterns are linked to particular words and allow for silent thought and reasoning without writing it down. Unlike other processing, they can report on their representations and modulate them on request. This unique property enables the model to think about concepts internally, which is not visible in its output.

articles.title: Evaluation order and nontermination in query languages
articles.summary: Evaluation order and nontermination in query languages are crucial aspects of functional programming that can lead to complex evaluation orders. A finite function can be represented as a key-value table whose keys are the function's support, which are inputs with non-default outputs. Non-boolean finite maps arise naturally from aggregations, while tensor algebra is a special case of relational algebra over these finite maps. The difficulty in evaluating such queries lies in determining their evaluation order, which can lead to nontermination and unexpected behavior.
